methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $100M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Climate & Energy (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 9 same-sector peers exist in Climate & Energy, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

data.narrative:
Xcimer Energy is a Series A climate & energy company founded by Conner Galloway and Alexander Valys that has raised $100M+. The valuation analysis draws on a comp universe of nine private companies and one public peer (LNZA) within the Climate & Energy sector, though same-stage and adjacent-stage peers proved insufficient for a robust median comparison—the private set includes established names like Helion, Redwood Materials, QuantumScape, KoBold Metals, and Electric Hydrogen.

With limited peer-comparable data at the narrow stage and valuation tier, the Stage-Progression method provides the primary analytical anchor. Using the $100M total raised as a proxy base value and applying a 2.0x step-up multiplier typical of Series A to next-stage progression, this method implies a valuation of $200M. This approach is applicable given the company's defined stage and capital history, though it relies on benchmark multipliers rather than transaction comps.

The triangulated valuation range is $170M to $230M, with a median of $200M. This single-method range reflects inherent uncertainty: the absence of sufficiently comparable same-stage or similar-tier peers in Climate & Energy limits confidence in peer-driven validation. Key uncertainties center on the appropriate step-up multiple for frontier-tech subsectors within climate, actual market conditions for Series A rounds in this space, and whether the $100M raised proxy accurately reflects fair-market equity valuation. Tighter estimates would require additional same-stage transaction data or a denser peer set.
